name: Ensure calling getWarnings() after JDBC write operations
description:
The rule applies when the write operation via following methods are used:

Statement/PreparedStatement/CallableStatement
1.Execute()
2.ExecuteUpdate()
3.ExecuteBatch()
4.ExecuteLargeUpdate()

ResultSet
1.UpdateRow()
2.InsertRow()
3.DeleteRow()
output:
Associated to each violation, the following information is provided:
- The number of violation occurrences
- Bookmarks for violation occurrences found in the source code
rationale:
JDBC drivers report non-fatal conditions as SQLWarning objects instead of throwing exceptions, so they never interrupt execution and are discarded unless the code explicitly calls getWarnings(). After an INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE, this channel commonly carries data-truncation notices (a value silently cut to fit its column), stored-procedure "success with info" messages, and — on DELETE — cascade or trigger side-effect warnings reporting rows affected beyond the direct delete.

Because the operation still reports success, skipping getWarnings() lets these signals vanish unnoticed — resulting in silent data corruption, incorrect row-count assertions, and lost audit trail entries that surface only later, when the damage is hard to trace.
reference:
IBM Db2 for Linux, UNIX and Windows - Handling an SQLWarning under the IBM Data Server Driver for JDBC and SQLJ
https://www.ibm.com/docs/en/db2/12.1.x?topic=ewudsdjs-handling-sqlwarning-under-data-server-driver-jdbc-sqlj

Oracle - The Java™ Tutorials - Retrieving Warnings
https://docs.oracle.com/javase/tutorial/jdbc/basics/sqlexception.html#retrieving_warnings

Microsoft JDBC Driver for SQL Server - SQLServerConnection methods
https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/sql/connect/jdbc/reference/getwarnings-method-sqlserverconnection?view=sql-server-ver17

Stack Overflow - When to call getWarnings() on Connections, Statements, and ResultSets with JDBC?
https://stackoverflow.com/questions/6434338/when-to-call-getwarnings-on-connections-statements-and-resultsets-with-jdbc
remediation: Regularly check conn.getWarnings() or stmt.getWarnings() after execution if data integrity is critical.
alternativeName: Ensure calling getWarnings() after JDBC write operations
